rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL BYKHOVSKY et al VS. WILLIAM PORTER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion To Compel Further Responses From Plaintiff (Part 2 Of 2 Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 1, DEFENDANT WILLIAM PORTER'S MOTION To Compel Further Responses From PlAINTIFF (PART 2 OF 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING.) Accordingly, defendant's motion to compel that category of information is granted. As to the second category the Court is unpersuaded that the need for the information relating to the way in the which the settlement amount was derived overrides the privileges that would protect the settlement process as between counsel and client. Accordingly, defendant's motion is granted to the extent that plaintiffs are ordered to supplement their responses to the special interrogatories on the topic to reflect the information provided in their opposition to defendant's motion. The remainder of the motion on this topic is denied. As to the third category related to communications between plaintiffs and their counsel and other lenders and their counsel, the court finds that a common interest does exists with respect to the adversarial position against defendant. Also defendant has not made a sufficient showing that any privileged communications are subject to disclosure here, and plaintiffs have represented that they will produce any information as to communications by plaintiffs other than with or through counsel, and plaintiff are ordered to produce any such information within five days of the date of this Order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL BYKHOVSKY et al VS. WILLIAM PORTER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Further Responses From Pltf
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 1, DEFENDANT WILLIAM PORTER'S MOTION To Compel Further Responses From PlAINTIFF (PART 1 OF 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING.) Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion is granted in part and denied in part. Defendant moves re four categories. Taking the last category first, the Court does not know why the plaintiffs have failed to provide the promised supplemental responses for over six months. However, plaintiffs represent they will provide these responses "upon this motion's resolution by the Court." Accordingly, plaintiffs are ordered to provide their supplemental responses to special interrogatory Nos. 87 and 88, and request for production Nos. 50, 56, and 59-61, with five days of the date of this Order. As to the first category related to the value of the property, it is premature as to this stage to assess the ultimate admissibility at trial of the evidence defendant seeks regarding the value of the property. SEE PART 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSE CASTILLO VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Order That 1)Facts In First Set Of Req. For Admissions Are Deemed Admitted 2) Provide Complete Answers To Form Interrogatories 2) Provide Complete Answers To Form Interrogatories 3) Etc
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 2, DEFENDANT PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY'S For Order That 1)Facts In First Set Of ReqUEST For Admissions Are Deemed Admitted 2) Provide Complete Answers To Form Interrogatories 2) Provide Complete Answers To Form Interrogatories 3) Etc. Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion is unopposed. Having reviewed the moving papers, the court grants defendant's motion as follows: Plaintiff is deemed to have admitted the Requests for Admission that defendant served on January 28, 2013. Plaintiff is ordered to provide full and complete responses to the form interrogatories and special interrogatories served by defendant on January 28, 2013. Plaintiff has waived any objections to these interrogatories. Plaintiff is ordered to produce all responsive, non-privileged documents to the Demand for Inspection of Documents served by defendant on January 28, 2013. Plaintiff has waived any objects to these document requests. All discovery responses and documents shall be provided to defendant no later than 15 days after the date of this order. Finally, the Court awards $450 in sanctions against plaintiff related to the failure to timely provide discovery responses. rulings.Case Title: JOESIAH BELL VS. 24 HOUR FITNESS USA, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 8, DEFENDANT MASS MOVEMENT, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The motion is denied in its entirety. Defendant failed to shift the burden of production. Defendant attaches the deposition of Mr. Jimenez to its moving papers and at pages 18-19 of the transcript, Mr. Jiminez indicates that defendant was the only contractor that performed installation work for 24 Hour Fitness USA, Inc. The acceptance doctrine does not apply to this case because the defect (lack of bolts) is fairly characterized as a latent defect. At the very least, there is a triable issue of fact regarding whether the defect was latent or patent. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: HBC ASSOCIATES LLC VS. LUCE FORWARD HAMILTON SCRIPPS, LLP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Moiton Andmotion To Specially Strike First Amended Complaint [Ccp 425.16]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 11, DEFENDANT MCKENNA LONG & ALDRIDGE LLP's motion To Specially Strike First Amended Complaint [Ccp 425.16]. The motion is denied. Defendant fails to show that this case is subject to CCP 425.16. "Legal malpractice is not an activity protected under the anti-SLAPP statute." Kolar v. Donahue, McIntosh & Hammerton (2006) 145 Cal.App.4th 1532, 1535. Whether Plaintiffs had an actual or implied-in-fact attorney client relationship with defendant is an issue to be determined in the underlying malpractice action. The two prior court orders relating to discovery disputes are not dispositive of this issue. Plaintiff's request for attorneys fees and costs pursuant to CCP 425.16(c )(2) is denied because the motion, although lacking in merit, is not frivolous. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: CINTYA GONZALEZ VS. MISSION NEIGHBORHOOD HEALTH CENTER, A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Motion For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 3, PLAINTIFF CINTYA GONZALEZ' Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Motion For Sanctions (PART 1 OF 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING). Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion is denied. Although plaintiff is correct that the notice of motion did not specify that defendant sought sanctions against plaintiff's counsel, in addition to plaintiff, defendant's moving papers did: "As a result of Plaintiff's abusive discovery tactics, Defendants request that this Court sanction Plaintiff and her attorney in the amount of $1,500, a fraction of the fees associated with preparing and filing this motion . . ." Fulton Dec., Ex 1B at 15:14-16. The Blumenthal court distinguishes the facts before it from the cases relied on by the trial court because where the "moving papers sought sanctions," counsel "were clearly placed on notice of the possible sanctions against them and the need to prove blamelessness." Id. Thus, this case is similar to those distinguished by Blumenthal because the moving papers put counsel on notice. SEE PART 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CINTYA GONZALEZ VS. MISSION NEIGHBORHOOD HEALTH CENTER, A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Motion For Sanctions (Part 2 Of 2 Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 3, PLAINTIFF CINTYA GONZALEZ' Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Motion For Sanctions (PART 2 OF 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING). Also the parties argued and the Court considered the issue of counsel's culpability at the hearing on the underlying motion. Defendant also sought the lesser alternative sanction of an adverse inference. Included in the argument section, on page 14, defendant observed "Issue sanctions in the form of jury instruction, which instruct the jury of a party's failure to produce documents, have also been authorized." The proposed order tracked this alternative. As to monetary sanctions, defendant put in the full amount expended and indicated that the $1,500 it sought in relief represented a "fraction" of that total amount. It was within the court's discretion to adjust the requested amount higher or lower. Plaintiff's due process argument is unavailing because plaintiff had notice as to the actual costs incurred. Finally, the motion is denied on procedural grounds. CCP 1008(a) requires that any motion for reconsideration be based on "new or different facts, circumstances or law." Plaintiff's motion does not offer new facts, at least ones that do not contradict plaintiff's testimony, and no new circumstances or law. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DIANE CONNELL VS. HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, INC., A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Pltf To Provide Further Answers To Her Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 4, DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S MOTION To Compel PlAINTIFF To Provide Further Answers To Her Deposition. Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion to compel further deposition testimony from the plaintiff is granted. Plaintiff has brought suit for, among other claims, fraud. Plaintiff testified that a representative of Defendant had made untrue statements. However, plaintiff's counsel would not permit questions designed to identify those statements and the basis for plaintiff's testimony that the statements were not true. These objections were improper. Contrary to plaintiff's argument, defendant's deposition questions were not impermissible contention interrogatories within the meaning of Rifkind v. Superior Court, 22 Cal. App. 4th 1255 (1994). In Rifkind, the deposing attorney sought the application of facts, and a listing of all them, to the legal contention of an affirmative defense. By contrast, here, defendant's counsel sought only factual testimony regarding facts about which plaintiff testified she knew. Nor are defendant's question "unfair" as plaintiff contends. The questions do not "call upon the deponent to sort out the factual material in the case according to specific legal contentions." Rather, the question quite properly ask the plaintiff for the factual basis of her assertions that certain statements were untrue when made to her, by asking her to identify those statements and any basis for claiming they are untrue. This line of questions is proper. Accordingly, defendant's motion is granted and plaintiff is required to appear for further deposition within 10 business days of this Order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

The deposition of defendant organization is properly noticed in San Francisco pursuant to CCP §2025.250(b) ("deposition of an organization that is a party to the action shall be taken at a place that is, at the option of the party giving notice of the deposition, either¿or within the county where the action is pending¿") National Union shall produce: (1) for deposition in San Francisco those of its officers, directors, managing agents, employees, or agents who are most qualified to testify on its behalf as to the matters designated in Plaintiff's "Notice of Deposition of Defendant National Union Fire Insurance Company of Pittsburgh, PA's Person(s) Most Knowledgeable re: Delivery of Policy" dated April 22, 2013; and (2) the documents designated in Exhibit A to Plaintiff's Notice. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion is granted. In support of its affirmative defenses and counterclaims, defendant served special interrogatories to discover the identity of the potential investors plaintiff says it had at the time of the potential sale. Plaintiff asserts that the discovery seeks irrelevant information, trade secrets, and invades the privacy of the potential investors. None of these arguments has merit. First, the investor identities are relevant to defendant's assertion that plaintiff had no reasonable basis for claiming that it had sufficient funds on hand to consummate the transaction. While the identities of the investors themselves will not prove or disprove defendant's defenses and counterclaims, the information is "reasonably calculated to lead to admissible discovery" because further factual development, which is possible only upon learning the identities, may show that plaintiff could not reasonably have believed that it could consummate this transaction with this group of potential investors. Second, it is not a trade secret within the meaning of Civil Code Section 3426.1(d). SEE PART 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: UN PLAZA LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY VS. UN PLAZA ASSOCIATES, LP A CALIFORNIA LIMITED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion To Compel Response To Special Interrogatories And Notice Of Request For Sanctions Of Attorney Fees Against Plaintiff (Part 2 Of 2 Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 12, 2013, Line 5, DEFENDANTS UN PLAZA ASSOCIATES, L.P. & HILL-UN PLAZA ASSOCIATES, INC.'S MOTION To Compel Response To Special Interrogatories And Notice Of Request For Sanctions Of Attoreney Fees Against PLAINTIFF (PART 2 OF 2 OF TENTATIVE RULING). Contrary to plaintiff's assertion that this information is tantamount to a customer list, in fact the evidence shows that this is, at best, a list of potential, not actual investors. Moreover, plaintiff has not made the requisite showing under CC 3426.1(d) that the identities meets the other criteria to qualify as a trade secret. Finally, even if the identities did qualify as a trade secret, the Protective Order in the case would adequately protect plaintiff's interests in maintaining confidentiality and ensuring that the information is used only for purposes of this litigation. Third, the information is not protected by any privacy interest that is not addressed by the Protective Order. Accordingly, plaintiff is ordered to fully respond to the special interrogatories no later than 10 days from the date of this Order. The Court denies the parties' cross-motions for sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
